title: The Door Left Open
description: Two colleagues who’ve spent months circling each other finally stop pretending—tonight, the adjoining door is open, and neither of them is leaving.

fullScenes.heading: Still
fullScenes.text:
Still. The hum of the minibar is the only sound in the suite, a low, steady pulse beneath the rain against the window. You’re sitting on the edge of the bed, your dress unzipped just enough to let the cool air touch your skin, when you hear it—the creak of the adjoining door, left ajar. Not wide. Just a sliver, enough to let the light from his room spill into yours. Enough to let you see the way his silhouette moves in the other space, the way he paces like a man who’s already made a decision he won’t admit to.

You don’t call out you don’t have to he knows you’re there. Ten years at the firm, and he’s never once left that door open by accident. Not after the awards dinner. Not after the whiskey. Not after the way his eyes lingered on the curve of your neck when you laughed at something that wasn’t funny.

"You left the door open," you say, voice steady, though your pulse isn’t. The words hang in the quiet, heavier than they should be.

He doesn’t turn right away. He takes his time, rolling up his sleeves another inch, the fabric whispering against his forearms. The dim light catches the gold of his watch, the way his fingers flex before he finally faces you. "Didn’t think you’d notice," he replies, and there’s something in his voice—something rough, something that wasn’t there in the boardroom. His tie is loose, his collar undone, and for the first time tonight, he looks like he’s done pretending.

You should look away. You don’t. "I always notice," you say, and the silence that follows is heavier than it should be. The rain picks up, tapping against the glass like fingers drumming on a table, waiting for someone to make the next move.

fullScenes.emotionalShift: curiosity gives way to something harder to name

fullScenes.heading: The Weight of a Glass
fullScenes.text:
The cold of the glass is still on your palm when he steps into the room, the ice clinking softly as he hands it to you. His fingers brush against yours—deliberate or not, you don’t pull away. The whiskey inside burns, but not as much as the way his thumb lingers for a second too long, the way his knuckles graze the inside of your wrist before he pulls back.

He’s watching you. Not the way he does in meetings, where his gaze is sharp and assessing. This is different. This is the way he looks at you when he thinks you’re not paying attention—the way his eyes track the movement of your throat when you swallow, the way his jaw tightens when you cross your legs.

"You’re quiet tonight," he says, handing you the glass. His voice is lower than it should be, rougher than it was an hour ago. The whiskey in your hand is the only thing keeping you grounded.

"And you’re not," you reply, taking a sip. The liquid warms your chest, but it does nothing for the heat pooling lower, the way your skin prickles under his gaze. "What do you want, James?"

He doesn’t answer right away. He steps closer, close enough that you can see the stubble along his jaw, the way his lips part when he exhales. "I want to know why you’re still here," he says, voice low. "After all these months of pretending."

The glass is still in your hand, the ice melting into the whiskey. You don’t drink. You don’t move. You just let the question sit between you, heavy and unanswered, while his fingers flex at his sides like he’s fighting the urge to reach for you again.

fullScenes.heading: Tell Me to Stop
fullScenes.text:
His hand closes around your wrist. Not gently. Not like the brush of his fingers earlier. This is a grip—firm, unyielding, the kind of touch that doesn’t ask permission. Your pulse jumps against his thumb, and for a second, you forget how to breathe.

"Tell me to stop," he says, voice rough. His eyes are dark, fixed on yours, and there’s something in them—something hungry, something that makes your chest tighten. "Say it now, or I won’t."

You should. You know you should. But the words don’t come. Instead, you swallow, your throat dry, and the only thing that escapes is the truth. "I don’t want you to stop," you admit, and the words hang between you, raw and undeniable. His grip tightens, just for a second, like he’s testing the weight of them.

Then his mouth is on yours. Not soft. Not hesitant. This is a kiss that doesn’t ask—it takes. His free hand slides into your hair, tilting your head back, and the taste of whiskey and something darker, something that’s just him, floods your senses. You don’t pull away. You don’t want to. The door is still open behind him, the rain still tapping against the window, but none of it matters. Not when his teeth graze your lower lip, not when his breath hitches against your skin.

He doesn’t answer. He doesn’t need to. His mouth is on yours before you can breathe again.

fullScenes.heading: On Your Knees
fullScenes.text:
The heat of his skin is the first thing you notice—the way it radiates off him, the way it seeps into you when he steps closer, his hands already reaching for the zipper of your dress. The fabric pools at your feet, and for a second, you’re standing there in nothing but your heels, the cool air raising goosebumps along your arms. His eyes darken, his breath catching as he takes you in, and the way he looks at you—like he’s been waiting for this, like he’s been starving for it—makes your knees weak.

"On your knees," he commands, voice low. His hands are already working at his belt, the metal clinking softly in the quiet room. "I want to feel your mouth on me."

You don’t hesitate. You sink to your knees in front of him, your hands sliding up his thighs, feeling the way his muscles tense under your touch. His cock is already hard, straining against the fabric of his boxers, and when you free him, the heat of him against your palm makes your breath hitch. He’s thick, heavy, the head already glistening, and when you lean in, your tongue swirling around the tip, his groan is all the encouragement you need.

"Like this?" you ask, taking him deeper, your lips stretching around him. His fingers tangle in your hair, not pulling, not yet, but the pressure is there, the promise of what’s coming. His hips jerk, just once, and the sound he makes—something between a curse and a prayer—sends a jolt of heat straight to your core.

"Fuck—yes," he gasps, his grip tightening. "Now ride me. I want to watch you."

He doesn’t give you time to think. His hands are on your waist, lifting you onto the bed, and then he’s flipping you onto your back, his body covering yours. "Turn over," he growls, his voice rough with need. "I want to see your face when I fuck you."

You don’t argue. You roll onto your stomach, your breath catching as he grips your hips, pulling you onto your knees. His cock slides against your wetness, teasing, and the way he feels—thick, hot, pressing against you—makes you whimper. "Please," you breathe, looking back at him. "I want you inside me. Now."

He doesn’t make you wait. He thrusts into you in one smooth motion, filling you completely, and the stretch—god, the stretch—makes your fingers dig into the sheets. His hands are on your hips, holding you in place, and the way he moves—slow at first, then harder, deeper—makes your vision blur. You can feel every inch of him, every ridge, every pulse, and when he leans over you, his breath hot against your ear, you know you’re not going to last.

"No—like this," you breathe, pushing back against him, taking control. You flip onto your back, pulling him with you, and then you’re straddling him, your hands on his chest as you sink down onto him again. His eyes darken, his hands gripping your thighs, and the way he looks at you—like he’s never wanted anything more—makes your breath catch. "I want to feel you," you say, rolling your hips, taking him deeper. "Don’t stop."

fullScenes.heading: The Door Is Still Open
fullScenes.text:
You’d rehearsed exactly what you’d say, and now you remember none of it. The sheets are tangled around your legs, the air thick with the scent of sex and whiskey, and the way his fingers trace slow circles on your hip makes it hard to think. The rain against the window is softer now, a quiet rhythm that matches the steady rise and fall of his chest beside you.

"We shouldn’t have done this," you say, though your body still hums with the memory of him. The words feel hollow, like they belong to someone else.

He doesn’t answer right away. His hand stills on your skin, his thumb pressing just hard enough to make you shiver. "Too late for regrets," he murmurs, his voice rough. "The door’s still open."

You don’t look at him. You don’t have to you can feel the weight of his gaze, the way his eyes linger on the curve of your shoulder, the way his breath hitches when you shift beside him. The adjoining door is still ajar, the light from his room spilling into yours, and the way it sits there—open, waiting—makes your chest tighten.

You don’t answer. You don’t have to. The silence says everything.
